criteria.summary: Provide text alternatives for non-text content
Provide an alternative to video-only and audio-only content
Provide captions for videos with audio
Video with audio has a second alternative
Live videos have captions
Users have access to audio description for video content
Provide sign language translations for videos
Provide extended audio description for videos
Provide alternatives for live audio
Semantic structure to make information and relationships conveyed through presentation programmatically determinable
The order of content presented to assistive technologies allows the user to make sense of the content
Use more than one sense for instructions. All users can access instructions for using the content, even when they cannot perceive shape or size or spatial location
Content can be display in portrait and landscape orientation
Each input field must be able to be determined programmatically, a user should be able for example to autofill inputs
Interface components, icons and landmarks (sections, article, main, etc.) must be able to be identified programmatically to help navigation for assistive technologies
Don’t use presentation that relies solely on colour
Don’t play audio automatically
Contrast ratio between text and background is at least 4.5:1
Text can be resized to 200% without loss of content or function
Don’t use images of text
Contrast ratio between text and background is at least 7:1
Audio is clear for listeners to hear
Offer users a range of presentation options
Don’t use images of text
User must be able to browse a website using a 320 pixel wide screen without having to scroll horizontally (There are some exceptions)
Extend color contrast of at least 3:1 to non-text content such as infographics, diagrams, states, etc.
Changing text style properties shouldn't break the page (line height, spacing after paragraph, letter spacing, word spacing)
Elements that are being shown on focus or hover (skip navigation, tooltip) should be dismissible(Esc), hoverable, persistent
Ensuring keyboard control for all functionality
Ensuring that users are not trapped in content
Same 2.1.1, but with no exceptions allowed
Providing a mechanism to allow users to remap or turn off character key shortcuts
Time limits have user controls
Provide user controls for moving content
No time limits
Don’t interrupt users
Save user data when re-authenticating
Users should be warned if user inactivity could cause data loss, unless data is preserved for more than 20h
No content flashes more than three times per second
No content flashes more than three times per second
Motion animation triggered by interaction can be disabled
Provide a ‘Skip to Content’ link
Use helpful and clear page titles
Logical order. When users navigate sequentially through content, they encounter information in an order that is consistent with the meaning of the content and can be operated from the keyboard
Every link’s purpose is clear from its context
Offer several ways to find pages
Use clear headings and labels
Ensure keyboard focus is visible and clear
Let users know where they are
Every link’s purpose is clear from its text
Section headings are used to organize the content.
Complex gesture (Pinch, zooming, swiping) should have a simpler gesture alternative (Tap, double taps, long press)
When using single pointer events, one of the following should be true, No Down-Event, Abort or Undo, Up Reversal, Essential
Text in buttons or label should be readable by assistant technologies and can be used with Text-to-speech
Functionalities trigger by moving the device should have a fallback without (Eg some apps use shake to undo)
The size of the target for pointer inputs is at least 44 by 44 CSS pixels
Inputs must to available to use with a different mechanism (Mouse, keyboard, stylus, touch, voice)
Page has a language assigned
Tell users when the language on a page changes
Explain any strange words
Explain any abbreviations
Users with nine years of school can read your content
Explain any words that are hard to pronounce
Elements do not change when they receive focus
Elements do not change when they receive input
Use menus consistently
Use icons and buttons consistently
Don’t change elements on your website until users ask
Clearly identify input errors. Ensure that users are aware that an error has occurred and can determine what is wrong.
Label elements and give instructions
Suggest fixes when users make errors.  Ensure that users receive appropriate suggestions for correction of an input
Reduce the risk of input errors for sensitive data
Provide detailed help and instructions
Reduce the risk of all input errors
Build all elements for accessibility
Content that is updated dynamically must be notified to users of assistive technologies without getting visual focus
When a UI component receives keyboard focus, at least part of it is not hidden by author-created content
All functionality that uses dragging can be achieved with single pointer without dragging, unless essential
Touch targets are at least 24x24 CSS pixels, with spacing exceptions
Help mechanisms repeated on multiple pages occur in same relative order
Information previously entered by user is auto-populated or available to select
Cognitive function tests not required unless alternatives provided or object recognition
When a UI component receives keyboard focus, no part of it is hidden by author-created content
Focus indicator has minimum size of 2px perimeter and 3:1 contrast ratio
Cognitive function tests not required for authentication except object/personal content recognition

testing_procedures.test_method:
Set screen reader focus to each non-text content element including images, buttons, form controls, iframes, and audio/video. 
Check that accessible name spoken is a short, accurate description of the non-text content, but does not identify its type. E.g. a play button should have the label “Play” but not its role “button” included in the label. Check that you don’t hear output similar to “Play Button, button”. 
Check that role spoken correctly describes element’s state, behavior, or usage. E.g. check that buttons have a button role and images have an image role. 
Check that any required accessible description information is also spoken.
Check that adjustable elements like sliders have the correct value like “50%”. Adjust the values and check that the screen reader speaks the correct value.
Check that purely decorative images are not focusable by the keyboard or screen reader. 
Check that that custom views such as star rating widgets speak an accurate label.

Check that information presented in audio-only format has a text transcript.
Check that information presented in video-only format has a either an audio equivalent or a text transcript.

Enable closed captions under Settings.
Play videos. 
Check that videos include synchronized captions.
Check that captions are accurate.

Play videos. 
Language button should appear if closed audio descriptions track included.
Activate Language button, select audio descriptions track.
Check that audio descriptions are accurate.
Check that live captions are provided for all real-time audio/video presentations.

Play videos. 
Language button should appear if closed audio descriptions track included.
Activate Language button, select audio descriptions track.
Check that audio descriptions are accurate.

Navigate through all semantic HTML elements using screen reader gestures or keyboard commands. 
Set screen reader focus to Headings, Lists, Tables and check that they correctly coded with semantic HTML.
If you cannot set focus to an item that appears to be a Heading element when using heading quick navigation commands then is is not coded with semantic HTML.
Navigate between row and columns inside data table cells with the screen reader and check that the correct row and column headers are spoken.
Check that the <legend> text is spoken for each <input> within a <fieldset> when the screen reader sets focus to an input in the group.
Use Landmark navigation to check that you can jump between the visually distinct Header, Navigation, Main Content, and Footer regions of the page.

Swipe through all elements on the screen from top to bottom and check that the reading order matches the visual presentation order.
Check that content is read in an order that does not disrupt the meaning.
Check that instructions provided for using the app do not rely solely on visual or audio-only information.

Set device to use grayscale colors only in accessibility settings.
Check that color alone is not used as the only means of conveying information.
Check that any audio that plays automatically for more than 3 seconds has a method to pause or stop the audio, or a method to control its audio volume independent from the system volume.

Use a color contrast analyzer testing tool to check that text is not too light when used on a light background or too dark when used on a dark background. 
Logos and text that are purely decorative do not need contrast testing. 
Disabled controls don't need to meet contrast requirements.

Pinch-to-zoom should be able to increase the text size.
If pinch-to-zoom is not working then another method must be available to resizes text.
Enable Large Text sizes under accessibility settings and test native app to check that text can enlarge.

Images of text should not be used.
Set focus to any images of text. 
Check that all images of text have the exact, correct text alternative.

For iOS, navigate the app and activate all user interface controls using VoiceOver and a Keyboard.
For Android, navigate the app and activate all user interface controls using a Keyboard only without TalkBack running.
Navigate through all elements in the app and check that focus is never trapped.

Navigate through the app but leave the app idle for a period of time until the time-out activates. 
Check that there is a warning before you are kicked out and that you are allowed to extend your session before being logged out. Or check that you can adjust the time limit settings to at least 10 times longer than the default. 
Time out extensions do not apply to real time exceptions like auctions or timed tests.
Check that moving, blinking, scrolling, or auto-updating content is able to be paused, stopped, or hidden.
Check that the app has no elements that flash more than three times per second.

Check that there is a method such as a skip link or WAI-ARIA landmarks that allow the user to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple screens.
Use Landmarks quick navigation commands to bypass blocks of content. Check that you can use landmarks to skip over the navigation directly to the main content.
Check that each screen in the app has a useful & unique title.
Navigate through all elements in the app using gestures or the keyboard and check that they are read in the correct order.

Set focus to every link and button, check that its accessible name spoken to the screen reader makes sense.
Check that the purpose of each link or button can be determined based on its label text alone or its label text together with its programmatically determined link context.
Check that there are multiple ways to find content within an app by using a search feature, table of contents, site map, or linking to all parts of app from the home page.
Check that headings used for organizing content and labels for user interface controls and form inputs describe their topic or purpose.

Navigate through all elements of the app using a keyboard and check that focus is always visible. 
Check that the currently focused element is always visible on screen.

Set screen reader speech settings to default language.
Set focus to page content and the screen reader should speak text in the proper speech language synthesizer.

Set screen reader speech settings to default language.
Set focus to parts of the page with a different language and the screen reader should speak text in the proper speech language synthesizer, e.g. lang="es" will speak with a Spanish screen reader voice rather than English.
Set focus to all components of the app and check that when a component receives focus that it does not cause a change of context.
Change the settings of all user interface components and check that does not automatically cause of a change of context unless the user has been warned of that behavior before using the component.
Navigate through all screens of the app and check that the app’s internal navigation occurs in the same relative order and is consistent.
Navigate through all screens of the app and check that the user interface components are identified consistently.

Submit forms in the app using blank and incorrect data.
Check that errors are identified, described in text, and spoken by the screen reader.
Set focus to inputs that have errors and check that the error messages are spoken by the screen reader.

Set focus to each text input control and check that the spoken label provides instructions for user input.
Set focus to form controls that require specifically formatted input and check that all instructions for correctly entering the text are spoken by the screen reader.
Check that labels and instructions are always visible to the sighted user also.

Set focus to an input that has been incorrectly formatted or was left blank and is in error. 
Check that an example or suggestion of a correct input and its format is visible and spoken by the screen reader.

This success criterion applies to apps with actions that cause legal commitments, financial transactions, that modify or delete user-controllable data in data storage systems, or submits user test responses. 
Test the app to check that submissions are reversible, that the user can check the date for input errors with a chance to correct them, or that information is reviewed, confirmed, and corrected before finalizing the submission.

Set focus to all user interface components including form elements, links, and JavaScript widgets and check that the accessible name spoken makes sense, and that the element has correct role, state, and value. 
Check that the role spoken (link, button, etc.) correctly matches the functionality of the element.
Check that the state of the element is spoken. E.g. expanded/collapsed/dimmed/disabled.
Check that the value of an adjustable element is accurate.

Navigate through all focusable elements using Tab key.
Check that when an element receives focus, at least part of it remains visible.
Test with common overlapping elements like sticky headers, cookie banners, chat widgets, and footers.
Verify that no author-created content completely hides the focused element.
Test on both desktop and mobile viewports.
Check that user can still interact with partially visible focused elements.

Identify all drag-and-drop functionality (sortable lists, sliders, file uploads, rearrange interfaces).
For each drag operation, verify a single-pointer alternative exists (buttons to move up/down, increment/decrement controls).
Test that alternatives work without requiring dragging gestures.
Check that functionality is preserved when using alternatives.
Verify exceptions: dragging is essential (e.g., freehand drawing) or determined by user agent.
Test on touch devices and with keyboard-only navigation.

Measure all interactive targets (buttons, links, form controls) using developer tools.
Verify targets are at least 24x24 CSS pixels.
For targets smaller than 24x24, check spacing: draw 24px diameter circles centered on each target.
Verify circles don't overlap other targets.
Check exceptions: inline links in sentences, user agent controls, targets where size is essential.
Test on actual touch devices, not just desktop browsers.
Pay special attention to mobile interfaces and touch-first designs.

Identify help mechanisms repeated across multiple pages (contact forms, phone numbers, chat widgets, help links, FAQs).
Navigate through multiple pages that include these help mechanisms.
Verify help mechanisms appear in same relative order on each page.
Check that position relative to other content remains consistent.
Verify exceptions: changes initiated by user, different page templates.
Test across different breakpoints and responsive layouts.
Check that visual position consistency matches DOM order.

Map out multi-step processes (checkout, registration, multi-page forms).
Identify information requested multiple times within same process.
Verify that previously entered information is either auto-populated or available for selection.
Test that data persists across form steps.
Check exceptions: re-entry essential for security, previous data no longer valid, user explicitly requests re-entry.
Test form autofill functionality.
Verify user can modify auto-populated data if needed.

Review all authentication steps (login, password reset, multi-factor authentication).
Identify cognitive function tests (remembering passwords, solving puzzles, transcribing distorted text).
For each cognitive test, verify at least one of these exists:
- Alternative method without cognitive test (biometric, email link, SMS code)
- Mechanism to assist completion (password manager support, paste enabled, show password)
- Test is object recognition (identifying common objects)
- Test is personal content (user's uploaded photos)
Test with password managers to ensure they work properly.
Verify paste is enabled in password fields.

Navigate through all focusable elements using Tab key.
Verify that when element receives focus, the ENTIRE element and its focus indicator are fully visible.
Check that NO part of the focused element is hidden by any author-created content.
Test with sticky headers, footers, modals, popups, and floating elements.
Verify across different viewport sizes and zoom levels.
Test that entire focus indicator is visible, not just the element itself.
This is stricter than 2.4.11 which allows partial visibility.

Navigate through interface using keyboard.
For each focus indicator, measure:
- Perimeter thickness (must be at least 2 CSS pixels)
- Contrast ratio between focused and unfocused states (must be at least 3:1)
Check that focus indicator is visible against all background colors.
Verify focus indicator meets size requirements: either 2px perimeter OR area equivalent to 2px perimeter.
Test exceptions: user agent default focus that author hasn't modified, background color unchanged by author.
Use color contrast analyzer tools to verify 3:1 ratio.

Review all authentication methods.
Verify NO cognitive function tests required at any step.
Check exceptions (same as 3.3.8 but fewer allowed):
- Alternative method exists
- Assistance mechanism provided
- Object recognition test
- Personal content test
Note: This is stricter than 3.3.8 - fewer exceptions allowed.
Test all authentication paths including password reset, account recovery.
Verify users never need to transcribe text, solve puzzles, or remember complex information.

resources.name: Mobile Accessibility: How WCAG 2.0 and Other W3C/WAI Guidelines Apply to Mobile
resources.notes: In case we want to make the mobile accessible as well

resources.name: Human Interface Design Guidelines
resources.notes: This is from Apple, relevant mostly for iOS

resources.name: Xcode Accessiblity Inspector
resources.notes: This is from Google, relevant mostly for Android

resources.name: Accessibility for iOS and tvOS
resources.notes: iOS stuff, may not apply to us as of now.

resources.name: Android Developer Resources
resources.notes: Anroid stuff, may not apply to us fully.

resources.name: Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act, 2005
resources.notes: KUDO is global, so this one may not apply at all

resources.name: What's New in WCAG 2.2
resources.notes: Official W3C overview of 9 new success criteria
